definition: Big-O notation describes how an algorithm's resource use grows as input size grows, focusing on the dominant growth pattern instead of exact runtime seconds.
deepExplanation: Big-O answers the question: what happens to the amount of work when n gets large? It ignores machine speed and small constant details so algorithms can be compared by growth rate. O(1) stays constant as n grows. O(log n) grows slowly because the problem shrinks by a factor each step. O(n) grows in proportion to the input. O(n log n) often appears in efficient divide-and-conquer sorting. O(n^2) often appears when code compares many pairs with nested loops.
whyItMatters: Big-O lets students predict whether code will scale before they run it on a huge input.
analogy: Imagine grading assignments. Checking the first paper only is O(1). Checking every paper is O(n). Comparing every paper with every other paper is O(n^2).

codeExamples.title: Common growth patterns in Java

walkthrough.text: first reads one array element, so the work does not grow with n. This is O(1).
contains may inspect every value once, so the worst-case work grows linearly. This is O(n).
printPairs has one loop inside another over the same input, so it performs about n times n operations. This is O(n^2).

codeExamples.title: Binary search as O(log n)
codeExamples.code:
while low <= high:
  mid = middle index
  if values[mid] == target: return mid
  if values[mid] < target: low = mid + 1
  else: high = mid - 1

walkthrough.text: The loop continues only while there is still a search range.
Each step discards about half of the remaining values, which creates logarithmic growth.

diagrams.title: Growth Rate Comparison
diagrams.caption: As input grows, higher-order growth rates become dramatically more expensive.

diagrams.title: Loop Pattern Table
nodes: Divide and merge: O(n log n)
diagrams.caption: Common code shapes often map to common Big-O categories.

commonMistakes.mistake: Treating Big-O as exact runtime seconds.
commonMistakes.whyItHappens: Students naturally connect performance to time on a laptop.
commonMistakes.correction: Use Big-O to describe growth as n changes; actual seconds also depend on hardware, constants, and implementation.

commonMistakes.mistake: Keeping constants and lower-order terms in the final answer.
commonMistakes.whyItHappens: The counting step may produce expressions like 3n + 10.
commonMistakes.correction: For Big-O, keep the dominant growth term, so 3n + 10 becomes O(n).

commonMistakes.mistake: Assuming every nested loop is automatically O(n^2).
commonMistakes.whyItHappens: Nested loops are a useful warning sign, but their ranges still matter.
commonMistakes.correction: Count how many times each loop can run; nested loops over different bounded ranges may not be n squared.

bestPractices: Analyze worst case unless the problem asks for best or average case.
Identify the dominant term after counting major operations.
Explain Big-O with code shape and input growth, not only a memorized label.

miniQuiz.question: What is the Big-O of one loop that may inspect every item once?
miniQuiz.explanation: One pass over n items grows in direct proportion to the input size.

miniQuiz.question: Why is Big-O not the same as runtime seconds?
options: It describes scaling as n changes
miniQuiz.answer: It describes scaling as n changes
miniQuiz.explanation: Big-O compares growth rates, while runtime seconds also depend on hardware, constants, and implementation details.

miniQuiz.question: What does O(log n) usually mean in an algorithm?
options: The problem size shrinks by a factor each step
The input must be a string
miniQuiz.answer: The problem size shrinks by a factor each step
miniQuiz.explanation: Binary search is O(log n) because each comparison removes about half of the remaining search space.

flashcards.back: How resource use grows as input size grows, focusing on the dominant growth rate.

flashcards.front: What is a common code shape for O(n^2)?
flashcards.back: Nested loops that each range over the same n-sized input.

flashcards.front: Why does binary search have O(log n) time?
flashcards.back: Each step discards about half of the remaining search space.
